section_name: Proposal for SIOE 2025: A Theory of Aristocracy
text: Authors: Jiahua Che, John K.-H. Quah, Tuan-Hwee Sng 1 A major theme in economic history research is the Great Divergence between East and West—how Western Europe and its offshoots in North America and Australasia came to surpass China, India, and other regions in terms of wealth and global influence.

section_name: Proposal for SIOE 2025: A Theory of Aristocracy
text: Scholars have offered various explanations for this divergence, ranging from political fragmentation and cultural attitudes to geographical endowment or luck.

section_name: Proposal for SIOE 2025: A Theory of Aristocracy
text: More recently, a growing body of research has emphasized that China’s early centralization and bureaucratization played a decisive role in shaping its long-term trajectory—fostering stability while potentially constraining institutional adaptability and technological innovation, ultimately resulting in long-term developmental stasis.

section_name: Proposal for SIOE 2025: A Theory of Aristocracy
text: China’s exceptionally long history of centralized statehood thus raises an important question in comparative economic development.

section_name: Proposal for SIOE 2025: A Theory of Aristocracy
text: As Fukuyama notes, China established a unified administrative system in 221 BCE—well before Augustus founded the Roman Empire—and maintained a cohesive imperial polity for much of the subsequent two millennia.

section_name: Proposal for SIOE 2025: A Theory of Aristocracy
text: At the heart of this system was a reliance on professional bureaucrats rather than feudal lords, ensuring that governance remained centralized and directly accountable to the state rather than fragmented among hereditary elites.

section_name: Proposal for SIOE 2025: A Theory of Aristocracy
text: It has long been recognized that imperial China’s bureaucratic apparatus was larger and more sophisticated than that of the contemporary Roman and Byzantine Empires.

section_name: Proposal for SIOE 2025: A Theory of Aristocracy
text: The same holds true when comparing China with its neighbors.

section_name: Proposal for SIOE 2025: A Theory of Aristocracy
text: Despite adopting key Chinese institutions such as the imperial examination system—whether briefly or for extended periods—Japan, Korea, and Vietnam never developed bureaucracies on the same scale as China’s.

section_name: Proposal for SIOE 2025: A Theory of Aristocracy
text: Why, then, was imperial China able to build and sustain such a vast bureaucratic structure while its contemporaries and neighbors developed only limited 1 Jiahua Che, China Europe International Business School.

section_name: Proposal for SIOE 2025: A Theory of Aristocracy
text: Email: jiahua.che @ gmail.com.

section_name: Proposal for SIOE 2025: A Theory of Aristocracy
text: John K.-H. Quah, Johns Hopkins University and National University of Singapore.

section_name: Proposal for SIOE 2025: A Theory of Aristocracy
text: Email: john.quah @ jhu.edu.

section_name: Proposal for SIOE 2025: A Theory of Aristocracy
text: Tuan-Hwee Sng, National University of Singapore.

section_name: Proposal for SIOE 2025: A Theory of Aristocracy
text: Email: tsng @ nus.edu.sg.

section_name: Proposal for SIOE 2025: A Theory of Aristocracy
text: administrative systems, if any at all?

section_name: Proposal for SIOE 2025: A Theory of Aristocracy
text: It is, in fact, perplexing that the practice of selecting administrative personnel based on merit emerged relatively late in most parts of the world.

section_name: Proposal for SIOE 2025: A Theory of Aristocracy
text: In the West, merit-based recruitment developed in tandem with the rise of nation-states between the 16th and 18th centuries.

section_name: Proposal for SIOE 2025: A Theory of Aristocracy
text: This delayed development is striking, particularly given that influential thinkers—from Plato and Mencius to Voltaire, Montesquieu, and Weber—consistently advocated for meritbased public office.

section_name: Proposal for SIOE 2025: A Theory of Aristocracy
text: Even in imperial China, where meritocratic recruitment has its roots in early imperial times, rulers continued to appoint kin or individuals with patrimonial ties— hereafter referred to as aristocrats—to key positions until the final years of the imperial period, a practice that persists today in the form of “princelings” in contemporary Chinese politics.

section_name: Proposal for SIOE 2025: A Theory of Aristocracy
text: If bureaucracy represents a more efficient and equitable system, why have rulers across diverse historical and contemporary contexts continued to favor kin and loyal associates in administrative appointments?

section_name: Proposal for SIOE 2025: A Theory of Aristocracy
text: One common explanation lies in biological and psychological factors.

section_name: Proposal for SIOE 2025: A Theory of Aristocracy
text: Fukuyama argues that nepotism is the default mode of governance, rooted in evolutionary predispositions that drive humans to channel resources toward kin.

section_name: Proposal for SIOE 2025: A Theory of Aristocracy
text: This kin bias is not unique to our species; similar patterns have been observed in other primates, where affiliative behaviors—such as grooming, coalition support, and food sharing—are preferentially directed toward close relatives.

section_name: Proposal for SIOE 2025: A Theory of Aristocracy
text: In this view, the emergence of meritocratic bureaucracy represents an effort to counteract these inherent biases—biases that can be mitigated but never entirely eliminated.

section_name: Proposal for SIOE 2025: A Theory of Aristocracy
text: Indeed, Fukuyama cautions that institutions based on merit should not be taken for granted, as historical episodes of political decay have often paved the way for a resurgence of rule by connection.

section_name: Proposal for SIOE 2025: A Theory of Aristocracy
text: However, there are salient features associated with meritocratic bureaucracy that cannot be easily explained within a purely sociobiological framework.

section_name: Proposal for SIOE 2025: A Theory of Aristocracy
text: One notable aspect is that bureaucracy tends to be more centralized than an aristocratic system.

section_name: Proposal for SIOE 2025: A Theory of Aristocracy
text: As Weber observed, “The bureaucratic structure goes hand in hand with the concentration of the material means of management in the hands of the master.” Moreover, territories administered by bureaucrats were typically located closer to the capital than fiefs governed by hereditary lords.

section_name: Proposal for SIOE 2025: A Theory of Aristocracy
text: A strictly sociobiological perspective would also imply that China’s early adoption of bureaucratic governance stemmed from a relative disregard for kinship relations, which appears to contradict Confucianism’s kin-centered emphasis.

section_name: Proposal for SIOE 2025: A Theory of Aristocracy
text: These observations suggest that, beyond biological or psychological impulses, additional factors—such as strategic considerations—also shaped the appointment of kin and trusted associates to key positions.

section_name: Proposal for SIOE 2025: A Theory of Aristocracy
text: Building on the seminal study of Weber and more recent work such as Allen and Voth and Xu, we argue from a rational choice perspective that the persistence of aristocratic governance—or rule by connection—is not solely a reflection of an innate human preference for kin.

section_name: Proposal for SIOE 2025: A Theory of Aristocracy
text: Rather, even if a ruler gains no intrinsic satisfaction from nepotism, there are practical incentives for creating hereditary privileges, granting them to these individuals, and positioning these individuals in influential roles.

section_name: Proposal for SIOE 2025: A Theory of Aristocracy
text: In other words, if aristocrats did not exist, rulers would have an incentive to create them.

section_name: Proposal for SIOE 2025: A Theory of Aristocracy
text: Central to our theory is a fundamental tension between loyalty and competence—both essential for effective governance yet inherently at odds.

section_name: Proposal for SIOE 2025: A Theory of Aristocracy
text: Maximizing competence requires appointing the best talent from each generation; however, officials without tenure have little incentive to safeguard public resources and may even misuse them.

section_name: Proposal for SIOE 2025: A Theory of Aristocracy
text: Conversely, awarding tenure and hereditary privileges fosters loyalty but limits the talent pool to a small, elite class.

section_name: Proposal for SIOE 2025: A Theory of Aristocracy
text: In short, merit-based selection constrains a ruler’s ability to provide long-term incentives and secure loyalty, while fostering loyalty often comes at the expense of competence.

section_name: Proposal for SIOE 2025: A Theory of Aristocracy
text: This trade-off lies at the heart of our loyalty–competence dilemma.

section_name: Proposal for SIOE 2025: A Theory of Aristocracy
text: Specifically, we build a model to examine the challenges a ruler faces in making administrative appointments to maximize regime survival.

section_name: Proposal for SIOE 2025: A Theory of Aristocracy
text: We conceptualize the ruler’s domain as a continuous spectrum of jurisdictions extending from the capital to the regime’s outer boundary.

section_name: Proposal for SIOE 2025: A Theory of Aristocracy
text: For each jurisdiction, the ruler must decide: (1) whether to appoint an aristocrat with hereditary privileges or a bureaucrat with established talent; (2) whether to manage public goods provision directly from the capital or delegate resource allocation to local administrators; and (3) how to allocate the national budget between aristocratic privileges and resources, including how to divide resources between direct provision and delegation.

section_name: Proposal for SIOE 2025: A Theory of Aristocracy
text: This simple framework yields several key insights.

section_name: Proposal for SIOE 2025: A Theory of Aristocracy
text: First, our model predicts that local resource management for public goods will be entrusted exclusively to aristocrats, who have a vested interest in the regime’s survival, while bureaucrats will retain only limited authority under strict oversight.

section_name: Proposal for SIOE 2025: A Theory of Aristocracy
text: This finding helps explain the observed relationship between bureaucratization and resource centralization noted by Weber and clarifies why bureaucratically administered territories were often located closer to the capital than hereditary fiefs, where proximity reduced monitoring costs.

section_name: Proposal for SIOE 2025: A Theory of Aristocracy
text: Second, our model highlights the role of technological and socioeconomic change in the transition from aristocracy to bureaucracy.

section_name: Proposal for SIOE 2025: A Theory of Aristocracy
text: In the absence of social leveling—which fosters cultural alignment and expands educational opportunities—or reliable methods for identifying high-ability individuals, regimes tend to rely predominantly on aristocrats.

section_name: Proposal for SIOE 2025: A Theory of Aristocracy
text: However, as education spreads and cultural shifts bring the ruled into closer ideological alignment with the ruling class, non-aristocrats are more likely to develop skills and expertise valuable to the ruler, allowing bureaucrats to gain representation at the expense of aristocrats.

section_name: Proposal for SIOE 2025: A Theory of Aristocracy
text: Yet, this is not the whole story.

section_name: Proposal for SIOE 2025: A Theory of Aristocracy
text: Improved bureaucratic selection enhances regime stability, which in turn reinforces aristocratic loyalty, making aristocrats more attractive to the ruler as well.

section_name: Proposal for SIOE 2025: A Theory of Aristocracy
text: Thus, although unlikely, circumstances may arise in which aristocrats secure a larger share of government roles even as bureaucratic competence improves. 2 In this way, our model suggests that the transition from aristocracy to bureaucracy is likely to be a protracted process—marked by gradual progress interspersed with occasional reversals—and underscores a tendency toward aristocratic or patrimonial resilience that challenges conventional modernization theory.

section_name: Proposal for SIOE 2025: A Theory of Aristocracy
text: Third, by incorporating insights from anthropological evolutionary psychology and interpreting the aristocracy as a “club” in which members must continually invest time and resources in social ties to preserve their privileges, our model suggests that factors such as territorial size and ethnic composition significantly shape a ruler’s choice between aristocrats and bureaucrats.

section_name: Proposal for SIOE 2025: A Theory of Aristocracy
text: The practical challenge of maintaining the cohesion— and thus the loyalty—of a large aristocracy suggests that expansive empires tend to favor greater bureaucratization.

section_name: Proposal for SIOE 2025: A Theory of Aristocracy
text: By contrast, a ruler from an ethnic minority distinct from the general population has stronger incentives to rely on aristocrats drawn from the same minority group, given the relative ease of securing their loyalty, which in turn results in a more limited bureaucracy.

section_name: Proposal for SIOE 2025: A Theory of Aristocracy
text: By providing an analytical framework for understanding a ruler’s choice between aristocrats and bureaucrats, we offer a new explanation for China’s early and enduring bureaucratization— an important driver of the Great Divergence, according to recent research.

section_name: Proposal for SIOE 2025: A Theory of Aristocracy
text: Building on a growing 2 This is loosely analogous to the well-known phenomenon whereby a decrease in the price of a good may, counterintuitively, reduce its demand if the substitution effect is outweighed by an opposing income effect.

section_name: Proposal for SIOE 2025: A Theory of Aristocracy
text: body of literature suggesting that the geography and ecology of the eastern Eurasian landmass fostered political unification south of the Mongolian Steppe and east of the Tibetan Plateau, we argue that the vast size of the resulting empire made it nearly impossible to maintain a cohesive aristocracy as a “club” capable of ensuring adequate aristocratic loyalty.

section_name: Proposal for SIOE 2025: A Theory of Aristocracy
text: As a result, the empire had strong incentives to promote bureaucratic governance wherever feasible and to create enabling conditions for the expansion of bureaucracy where these conditions were lacking.

section_name: Proposal for SIOE 2025: A Theory of Aristocracy
text: Our theory also provides temporal insights into why bureaucratization in China tended to be more extensive under Han Chinese dynasties than under those ruled by ethnic minorities, as well as spatial insights into where bureaucratic jurisdictions and aristocratic fiefs were likely to concentrate.

section_name: Proposal for SIOE 2025: A Theory of Aristocracy
text: Additionally, our model provides a theoretical scaffold that both clarifies and refines Weber’s seminal work on bureaucratization.

section_name: Proposal for SIOE 2025: A Theory of Aristocracy
text: Despite its influence, Weber’s analysis has been criticized for lacking a clear overarching theory to unify observations that can sometimes appear broad or loosely connected.

section_name: Proposal for SIOE 2025: A Theory of Aristocracy
text: In particular, our model reproduces and explains some of Weber’s key insights—such as China’s early yet partial bureaucratization (akin to the Roman and Byzantine empires), the fundamental role of technological and socioeconomic forces, and direct control as a defining feature of bureaucratic rule—while also strengthening and illuminating how these insights are interconnected through formal analysis.

section_name: Proposal for SIOE 2025: A Theory of Aristocracy
text: Finally, we contribute to the literature on the loyalty–competence trade-off in governance.

section_name: Proposal for SIOE 2025: A Theory of Aristocracy
text: Existing research often argues that leaders may favor less competent subordinates to protect their own position, since more capable officials are better positioned to challenge or replace them.

section_name: Proposal for SIOE 2025: A Theory of Aristocracy
text: We propose a different perspective in which loyalty stems from dependence rather than incompetence and fluctuates with the subordinate’s perception of regime stability.

section_name: Proposal for SIOE 2025: A Theory of Aristocracy
text: This reinterpretation sheds new light on patrimonialism in history and offers potential insights into the enduring relevance of favoritism, cronyism, and nepotism in modern society.
